# Harms Framework

| Harm | Who is harmed | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Consumer deception | customers, viewers, patients, voters, fans | They mistake engineered proof for independent evidence |
| Creator displacement | original creators, honest operators | Synthetic volume crowds out organic discovery |
| Market pollution | platforms, advertisers, marketplaces | Ranking and recommendation systems learn from corrupted signals |
| Capital misallocation | investors, employees, customers | Inflated metrics redirect money and prestige |
| Measurement corruption | businesses, growth teams | Teams optimize toward fake leads, invalid traffic, vanity metrics |
| Trust erosion | everyone | Users stop believing that anything is organic or independent |
| Labor invisibility | clippers, editors, VAs, moderators | “Organic creator success” hides distributed production systems |
| Legal/regulatory cost | institutions, platforms | Enforcement must detect hidden coordination and attribution |
| Competitive unfairness | honest businesses | Deceptive actors gain ranking/visibility advantages |
| Cultural distortion | public discourse | Manufactured trends crowd out genuine signal from real communities |

## Key argument

The strongest harm is systemic: manufactured traction degrades the reliability of the systems people use to allocate attention and trust.
